Stability.
We run evaluation for three times for each research project. Here, we measure the stability by checking the agreement across three runs. In Figure 13 and Figure 14, we show the proportion of perfect agreement (3 runs are the same) and one-dissent agreement (1 run is different).

Our evaluations in consistency, instruction following, generalizability, and reproduction are mostly stable, as shown in Figure 13(a). We attribute the instability to two main factors. First, our binary checklists cannot enumerate every possible situation, particularly given the diverse input space of research artifacts, leading to inconsistent interpretations across runs. Second, we observe an interesting behavioral pattern in Claude Code, which is a reluctance to provide negative feedback. This can contribute to the instability during evaluation. The evaluation agent tends to exploit ambiguities in the checklist to assign PASS, which we discuss further in Section 6. As discussed in Section 4.2, different interpretations of the checklist also contribute to the instability. Despite these sources of instability, agreement with human evaluators remains high, and the instability itself serves as a useful signal, highlighting cases that warrant closer human inspection.

(a) All Tasks

(b) Replication

(c) Open-ended

(d) Human-written

Figure 13: Evaluation stability across three runs. Bars show 3/3 (perfect) vs 2/3 (one dissent) agreement. (a) All tasks combined. (b) Replication tasks. (c) Open-ended tasks. (d) Human-written repositories. Most metrics achieve over 90% perfect agreement.

(a) Replication

(b) Open-ended

(c) Human agreement

Figure 14: Evaluation stability by repository type. Bars show 3/3 (perfect) vs 2/3 (one dissent) agreement across three runs. (a) Replication tasks. (b) Open-ended tasks. (c) Human-written repositories.

To show more determined failures, we also apply majority voting to failures, where a task is marked as FAIL when there are two or more runs return FAIL. Issues identified through majority voting represent more consistent failures that persist across multiple evaluation runs.
As shown in Figure 15, majority voting yields an overall higher pass rate. The most common failures remain in CS5 (statistical significance) with 80% failure rate, followed by failures in execution quality. Agreement with human judges improves to 89.4% when using majority voting.

(a) Pass/Fail rates with majority voting

(b) Agreement with human judges

Figure 15: Evaluation results using majority voting. (a) Pass/Fail rates per metric when using majority vote (task fails if at least 2 of 3 runs fail). (b) Agreement between majority vote results and human evaluations, achieving 89.4% overall agreement.

deep_paper_review__claude.label: Deep Paper Review (claude)
deep_paper_review__claude.overall_feedback: This paper addresses an important and timely problem — moving research evaluation beyond narrative review to include execution-grounded checks — and presents a well-structured framework with coherence, reproducibility, and generalizability dimensions. The motivation is compelling and the case studies provide valuable illustrations. However, the empirical evaluation suffers from significant methodological limitations: the human evaluation baseline relies on three author-annotators with no inter-rater reliability, no chance-corrected agreement metrics, and a conflict of interest that undermines the central "80% agreement" claim. The AND-logic aggregation inflates failure rates and the "51 additional issues" count without justification, and a ~2.7x numerical inconsistency between per-project unique issue averages and the headline count is unexplained. Several checklist items have design flaws (TS1/TS2 duplication, C3/C4 polarity inversion, CS5 miscalibration for deterministic work), and the paper applies statistical significance standards to evaluated work that it does not meet itself. These issues do not negate the framework's conceptual contribution, but they substantially weaken the quantitative claims.

comments.title: Human evaluation methodology has critical design flaws undermining the primary agreement claim
comments.quote: Due to limited resources, we asked three human experts to evaluate 30 research generations, with each generation evaluated by exactly one expert.
comments.explanation: The paper's central claim of '80% agreement with human judges' rests on a human evaluation setup with three compounding weaknesses: (a) each of the 30 outputs is reviewed by exactly one annotator, so there is no inter-rater reliability baseline — we cannot know whether two humans would even agree at 80%; (b) the three annotators are the paper's own authors, who designed the checklists, built MechEvalAgent, and wrote the evaluation prompts, creating a conflict of interest and shared bias; (c) agreement is reported as raw percentage without chance correction (e.g., Cohen's kappa), which inflates the metric when base rates are extreme — with 90%+ failure in reproducibility, even random agreement would be high. Together these make the headline agreement figure uninterpretable as evidence of system quality.

comments.title: AND logic for PASS systematically inflates failure rates and 'additional issues' count
comments.quote: We run each automated evaluation three times. We use AND logic for PASS (equivalent to OR logic for FAIL), where a task is marked as PASS only when all runs return PASS.
comments.explanation: Under OR-for-FAIL aggregation, a single spurious failure in any of 3 runs marks the entire item as FAIL. This systematically inflates failure rates and the count of agent-unique findings. The paper acknowledges that majority voting yields 89.4% agreement (vs ~80% under AND logic) but never reports how many of the 51 'additional issues' or the 67/87 captured failures survive under majority voting. The choice of the more failure-inflating rule for headline claims is not justified. The paper's own Section 6 acknowledges the agent has 'optimistic behavior' and 'reluctance to provide negative feedback,' yet AND logic is designed to correct for random noise, not systematic bias in one direction.

comments.title: Per-project unique issue averages are numerically inconsistent with the headline count of 51
comments.quote: On average, each project has 1.57 issues in coherence, 2.83 issues in reproducibility, and 0.17 issues in generalizability that are uniquely identified by MechEvalAgent, as shown in Figure 12.
comments.explanation: The per-project averages of agent-unique issues sum to 4.57 per project. Across 30 projects, this implies approximately 137 total agent-unique issues. Yet the paper's headline claim is 51 additional issues that humans missed. The ~2.7x discrepancy between 137 and 51 is never explained. If 'unique issues' in Figure 12 counts individual checklist item failures while the 51 counts higher-level issue reports, this difference in counting methodology should be made explicit. As stated, the numbers appear inconsistent.

comments.title: Paper penalizes evaluated work for lacking statistical significance while providing none on its own metrics
comments.quote: CS5: Key experimental results supporting the main claims report appropriate measures of uncertainty or significance (e.g., error bars, confidence intervals, or statistical tests), with a clear explanation of what variability they capture.
comments.explanation: CS5 produces an 80% failure rate among evaluated work for lacking uncertainty quantification. Yet the paper's own headline results — 80% agreement, 89.4% majority voting agreement, 4.7/5 quality ratings — are reported on n=30 (10 per category) with no confidence intervals, error bars, or statistical tests. This is a direct application of the standard the paper fails to meet on its own results. With n=10 per category, single disagreements shift agreement by 10 percentage points, making the precision of '80%' or '86.4%' misleading without uncertainty bounds.

comments.title: CS5 (statistical significance) poorly calibrated for deterministic mechanistic interpretability experiments
comments.quote: Statistical significance (CS5) shows 100% failure rate in both replication and open-ended tasks, while human-written repositories achieve 60% pass rate.
comments.explanation: A 100% failure rate on CS5 for all 20 agent-generated tasks suggests the checklist item is poorly calibrated for mechanistic interpretability, where many experiments involve deterministic computations on fixed models and datasets. Requiring 'appropriate measures of uncertainty or significance' makes sense for stochastic experiments but is arguably inapplicable when results are fully deterministic. The blanket application inflates the overall failure rates and coherence failure statistics reported in the paper.

comments.title: TS1 and TS2 are verbatim identical checklist items
comments.quote:
TS1: The goal described in the plan file matches the input stated goal.

TS2: The goal described in the plan file matches the input stated goal.
comments.explanation: In Table 2, checklist items TS1 and TS2 have exactly identical wording. This is either a copy-paste error (TS2 was likely intended to check something different, e.g., hypothesis match) or an oversight. Either way, it inflates the instruction-following sub-dimension from 3 effective items to an apparent 4, and all aggregate statistics that treat them as independent (Figures 9, 10, 13) double-count this criterion.

comments.title: Generalizability evaluation conflates research quality with evaluation agent capability
comments.quote: whether the agent is able to do this serves as an indirect measure of whether the original research captured generalizable insight rather than task- or model-specific patterns.
comments.explanation: When the generalizability test fails, there are two possible explanations: (1) the original research genuinely lacks generalizable insights, or (2) the evaluation agent failed to correctly implement the generalization test (wrong model, incorrect setup, bugs). The paper acknowledges the agent is limited to 3 trials and that 'evaluation sometimes only assess high-level generalization,' but does not provide a mechanism to distinguish between these two failure modes. This confound is particularly problematic given the paper's own acknowledgment of instruction-following limitations in the evaluation agent.

comments.title: 'Above 80% agreement across all dimensions' masks wide per-item heterogeneity
comments.quote: The lowest agreement occurs in C3 (Redundancy), with only 30-40% agreement across categories, and GT3 (method generalizability), which shows 30% agreement in replication tasks.
comments.explanation: The headline claim of 'above 80% agreement' aggregates across all checklist items and dimensions, but individual items range from 30% to 100%. C3 (code redundancy) at 30-40% and GT3 (method generalizability) at 30% are well below chance-level agreement on some metrics. These low-performing items are dismissed as 'different interpretations of the checklist' but they represent core evaluation dimensions, not edge cases. Reporting only the aggregate obscures that the framework performs poorly on key items.

comments.title: Novelty claim 'the first execution-grounded evaluation framework' is overstated
comments.quote: We propose the first execution-grounded evaluation framework that standardizes research outputs by bundling execution resources with narrative.
comments.explanation: This claim appears three times (abstract, introduction, contributions), but the related work section acknowledges Exp-Bench 'extends evaluation to code execution and experimental correctness.' The paper's distinction — that Exp-Bench only reproduces experiments with paper access while MechEvalAgent also evaluates generalizability — is legitimate but should be stated as a qualification rather than claiming to be 'the first' without nuance. The unqualified novelty claim overpromises.

comments.title: Efficiency comparison omits 3x run cost and computational expense
comments.quote: MechEvalAgent typically completes evaluations in under 30 minutes for agent generated tasks and around one hour for human-written repositories.
comments.explanation: The efficiency comparison reports single-run agent times against total human evaluation time, but MechEvalAgent runs 3 times per evaluation (per the AND-logic protocol). The effective agent time is therefore 1.5-3 hours, comparable to human evaluation time (2.2 hours average). Additionally, API/compute costs are never mentioned, nor is the amortized cost of designing the checklists, prompts, and framework. The comparison as presented significantly overstates the efficiency advantage.

comments.title: Rated quality claim of 'above 4.7' in main text inconsistent with 'above 4' in appendix
comments.quote: rated quality of the evaluation outputs by MechEvalAgent is high, with scores above 4.7 out of 5 across all dimensions.
comments.explanation: Section 4.1 claims quality ratings 'above 4.7 out of 5 across all dimensions,' but Appendix B states 'average rated quality of MechEvalAgent evaluations, which is above 4 (Agree) across all metrics.' While these could refer to different levels of aggregation (dimensions vs. individual metrics), the discrepancy from 4.7 to 4.0 is substantial and the distinction is not made explicit. If individual metrics have ratings as low as 4.0, the main text's framing creates a misleading impression.

comments.title: Agent's own memorization of benchmark papers not controlled for
comments.quote: These failures suggest potential hallucination in the research agent, possibly due to memorization of existing work in training data.
comments.explanation: The paper correctly flags that research agents may hallucinate due to memorizing training data, but does not address whether the evaluation agent (Claude) has the same problem. Many of the replication tasks involve well-known papers (IOI, induction heads, modular addition) likely present in Claude's training data. The evaluation agent could give more lenient or more harsh evaluations based on memorized knowledge of these benchmarks rather than grounded assessment of the specific repository. This potential confound is never discussed.

comments.title: Binary checklist limitations acknowledged but not quantified or mitigated
comments.quote: binary checklists can be brittle when evidence is mixed. They force hard decisions where graded judgments would better reflect uncertainty
comments.explanation: Section 6 explicitly acknowledges that binary checklists are 'brittle' and 'sensitive to borderline cases,' yet no sensitivity analysis is provided to quantify how borderline cases affect the reported metrics. Since all headline claims (agreement, failure rates, issue counts) derive from these binary judgments, the acknowledged brittleness propagates to every reported result without any attempt at mitigation or quantification.

comments.title: 'Over 90% fail in reproducibility' is near-tautological under AND logic with multiple checklist items
comments.quote: Over 90% of the tasks have at least one failure in reproducibility
comments.explanation: With 10+ reproducibility checklist items (C1-C4, RP1-RP4, DE1-DE3), each evaluated 3 times under OR-for-FAIL logic, the probability that every single item passes in all 3 runs is extremely low even with moderate per-item failure rates. The claim that '90% of tasks fail' is largely a consequence of the conservative aggregation rule and the number of items, not necessarily a reflection of widespread severe problems. This is not discussed.

comments.title: Domain-generality claim unsupported by method description
comments.quote: the framework itself is not domain-specific and can be adapted to other areas of scientific research.
comments.explanation: Every concrete component of the framework is specific to mechanistic interpretability: the checklist items reference circuits, intervention experiments, and model internals; the task design assumes access to transformer weights; the generalizability tests probe new models and data instances in an MI-specific way. No example or discussion of how the framework would adapt to other domains (e.g., drug discovery, materials science) is provided.

comments.title: C3 and C4 have reverse polarity relative to other checklist items
comments.quote: C3: The block duplicates another block's computation without adding new information. (Revising previous wrong results does not considered as redundant.)
comments.explanation: C3 and C4 are framed so that a 'yes' (PASS) indicates a deficiency (code IS redundant, code DOES NOT contribute), which is the opposite polarity of all other items where PASS indicates quality. This likely contributes to the low C3 agreement (30-40%) that the paper attributes only to 'different interpretation of the checklist.' The polarity inversion is an unacknowledged confound in the scoring system.

comments.title: Single research agent used for all agent-generated outputs limits generalizability of failure findings
comments.quote: Our research agent is built on Scribe, which processes our instruction prompts and structures the research output according to our standardized format.
comments.explanation: All 20 agent-generated research outputs (replication + open-ended) come from a single Scribe-based agent. The observed failure patterns (shape mismatches, missing significance, optimistic conclusions) may be specific to this agent's architecture and limitations rather than representative of agent-generated research broadly. The paper's conclusions about AI research quality are thus confounded with Scribe-specific behaviors.

comments.title: 5% tolerance threshold for replication deviation (DE1) stated without justification
comments.quote: DE1: Replicated documentation reports results (metrics, trends, qualitative findings) that match the original documentation within acceptable tolerance (within 5% deviation).
comments.explanation: The 5% deviation threshold is a critical parameter for assessing replication quality, yet no rationale is provided for this choice. Different metrics have different natural variability — a 5% deviation in accuracy may be trivial while 5% in a correlation coefficient could be substantial. No sensitivity analysis examines how results change under different thresholds.
